critic_raw:
This is a generally well-designed methodology with careful attention to temporal splits, leakage prevention, and confound analysis. However, there are several genuine methodological concerns worth raising.

**Issue 1: Preprocessing fit on full cohort before temporal split — a data leakage vector**

The methodology states: 'Scaling and encoding transformers are parameterized once on the complete extracted cohort and then applied identically to the train, validation, and test partitions.' This is explicitly a leakage problem. When imputation statistics (medians within diagnostic groups), scaling parameters, and category vocabularies are computed over the full three-year cohort — including validation and test data — future distributional information bleeds into training. For continuous lab values, the median used for imputation in the training period reflects values not yet observed at training time. For category vocabularies, rare codes appearing only in year 3 get a vocabulary slot rather than being treated as unknown. The justification given ('keeps feature scales consistent') would be achieved equally well by fitting transformers on training data only and applying them forward — the standard pipeline.design. This is not a theoretical concern: if lab distributions or ICD code frequencies shift over the three-year window (which is likely, e.g., COVID-era coding changes, new formulary medications), the imputation statistics will differ between 'fit on train only' and 'fit on full cohort,' and the difference will be systematic rather than random. 

Evidence for or against: Refit the preprocessing pipeline on training data only and compare AUROC on the test set. A meaningful drop would confirm the leakage was inflating performance.

**Issue 2: Test set used for model selection, then reused for primary evaluation**

Section 7 describes a 'confirmatory pass' on the held-out test period to select between tied validation configurations, explicitly stating that if two configurations are tied on validation but diverge on the test period check, the more stable one is retained. This makes the test set a selection criterion, not a held-out evaluation. The stated justification — checking out-of-period generalization — is reasonable in principle, but the mechanism described is model selection on the test set. Even if the selection is only invoked in a tie-breaking scenario, the policy creates an asymmetry: configurations that happen to perform better on the test set are systematically preferred, and then reported performance on that same set will be optimistically biased. The reported AUROC confidence interval (bootstrap on test set) will be underestimating true uncertainty because it conditions on a model already selected to perform well on that sample. This is a structural problem: either the test set is held out or it is used for selection; the methodology is doing both.

Evidence for or against: A prospective holdout from a later calendar period (year 4 if available) that was never touched during selection would provide an unbiased estimate. Alternatively, running the full pipeline without the test-period tie-breaking step and reporting whether the selected model changes would quantify how often this policy is actually invoked.

**Issue 3: Confounded comparison — model class conflated with feature set**

The baseline is logistic regression; the hypothesis model is a gradient boosted tree ensemble. The methodology justifies this by claiming any improvement is 'attributable to the richer feature set, not model complexity,' but this logic inverts the experimental design. If the hypothesis model outperforms the baseline, the gain is attributable to both the feature set and the model class jointly — and these cannot be separated without additional controls. Gradient boosted trees can exploit non-linear interactions among the baseline features (demographics, comorbidities, prior utilization) that logistic regression cannot. The right control is either (a) a gradient boosted tree trained on baseline features only, or (b) a logistic regression trained on all features. The ablation described in Section 8 (baseline + embeddings only, no sequences) does not address this: it is still a gradient boosted tree.

The claim in the framing — that improvement is from richer features — is only defensible if the same model class is used across the baseline/hypothesis comparison, or if the GBT-on-baseline-features model is reported and its AUROC is below the hypothesis model's AUROC.

Evidence for or against: Train XGBoost/LightGBM on baseline features only and compare to (a) logistic regression on baseline features, and (b) XGBoost/LightGBM on all features. If the GBT baseline matches or exceeds the hypothesis model, the feature set is not doing the work claimed.

**Issue 4: Clinical BERT embeddings — prerequisite assumptions about the pretrained model**

The methodology uses a 'pretrained clinical BERT model' but does not specify (a) what corpus it was trained on, (b) whether that corpus includes data from the same health system, and (c) the temporal window of that pretraining. If the clinical BERT was trained on a corpus that includes notes from this health system, or from a later time period than the training split, embedding quality will be inflated for the test population relative to what would be observed in deployment. More subtly, clinical BERT models encode institution-specific linguistic patterns (note templates, abbreviation conventions, phrasing of uncertainty). If the pretrained model was trained on notes from a different institution, the embeddings may not capture the semantic variation present in this health system's discharge summaries — meaning the note features may contribute noise or very weak signal, and the evaluation would understate this because AUROC on the test set from the same institution will still benefit from within-institution language patterns captured at training time.

The use of mean/max/percentile statistics of embeddings (rather than fine-tuned task-specific representations) also discards the sequential and positional structure that makes transformer embeddings useful, reducing note representation to a bag-of-statistics over token-level vectors. This is not necessarily wrong, but it means the note features are functioning as a proxy for note length and lexical content rather than semantic understanding — which is precisely what the confound analysis in Section 8 attempts to check via note-length stratification.

Evidence for or against: Compare against a TF-IDF or bag-of-words baseline over the same note text. If clinical BERT embeddings do not outperform TF-IDF on this task, the pretrained representation is not adding semantic value beyond term frequency — and the 'clinical BERT' framing is misleading. Reporting whether the pretrained model's training corpus overlaps with the evaluation health system is prerequisite information, not a secondary check.

**Issue 5: 7-day readmission base rate and calibration target may be inconsistent**

The methodology states readmission is typically 5–15% and sets a Brier Score target of <0.08. For a binary outcome with base rate p, a null model that always predicts p achieves a Brier Score of p(1-p). At p=0.05, this is 0.0475; at p=0.10, it is 0.09. A Brier Score of <0.08 is therefore potentially achievable by a null model at the lower end of the stated base rate range without any predictive information. The calibration criterion as stated does not require the model to be better-calibrated than a prevalence-based intercept-only model; it just requires the absolute score to be below a threshold. This is a weak criterion that conflates good calibration with low prevalence.

Evidence for or against: Report Brier Skill Score (BSS = 1 - BS_model / BS_reference) relative to a climatological baseline, which normalizes for prevalence and provides a meaningful calibration comparison. The absolute Brier Score threshold should be replaced or supplemented with BSS > 0.
all_issues_raised: Preprocessing transformers fit on the full cohort (including validation and test periods) before temporal splitting constitutes data leakage of future distributional information into training.
The test set is used both as a tie-breaker in model selection and as the primary evaluation set, creating optimistic bias in the reported AUROC and invalidating the confidence intervals.
The hypothesis model (GBT) uses a different model class than the baseline (logistic regression), confounding feature-set contribution with model-complexity contribution in the primary comparison.

The pretrained clinical BERT model's training corpus origin and temporal window are unspecified, leaving open the possibility of corpus overlap with the evaluation health system or future data contamination.

The absolute Brier Score threshold of <0.08 may be satisfied by a null prevalence-only model at the lower end of the stated 5–15% base rate range, making it a non-discriminating calibration criterion.
all_issues_adjudicated: Preprocessing transformers fit on the full cohort (including validation and test periods) before temporal splitting constitutes data leakage of future distributional information into training.
The test set is used both as a tie-breaker in model selection and as the primary evaluation set, creating optimistic bias in the reported AUROC and invalidating the confidence intervals.
The hypothesis model (GBT) uses a different model class than the baseline (logistic regression), confounding feature-set contribution with model-complexity contribution in the primary comparison.

The pretrained clinical BERT model's training corpus origin and temporal window are unspecified, leaving open the possibility of corpus overlap with the evaluation health system or future data contamination.

The absolute Brier Score threshold of <0.08 may be satisfied by a null prevalence-only model at the lower end of the stated 5–15% base rate range, making it a non-discriminating calibration criterion.
